Very good. All right. Good morning, Minister.
Welcome along to the Foreign Affairs Defence Trade Committee.
We are very excited to be conducting the annual review for the-
(clears throat)
... Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade today.
Uh, and we welcome your presence with us this morning for 30 minutes to
give us some oversight in your role as Associate Minister on behalf of the
Minister, but obviously also as Minister of Trade and Investment too, I'm sure you
have questions.
So, I'd like to hand over to you for some introductory comments and then
there will be questions from members around the room. Thank you.
Well, thank you Mr. Chair. Thank you. And committee members.
I'm pleased to appear before the committee and I extend apologies from
the, the, uh, Minister of Foreign Affairs and Trade, the Right Honourable Winston
Peters, who's unable to join us today.
Advancing and protecting New Zealand's national interests in this more
contested, fragmented and volatile world requires
determination and focus. Over the past year, the government has
responded to an increasingly challenging international environment with
energy, resolve and with hard work.
Uh, we have sustained a high tempo of international engagement, engagement at
senior levels across government, focused on advancing the security and
prosperity for all New Zealanders.
Minister Peters' travel, along with that of the Prime Minister, the Minister of
Defence, the Minister of Climate Change and myself has involved 88
high-level visits to 43 countries over the past
year. This outward engagement was, um, supplemented by hosting
62 high-level visits to New Zealand by foreign leaders and
ministers, showcasing the best our country has to
offer and what we can offer to the world.
Uh, collectively, this investment in building the international
connections, uh, at a political, at a political level is a clear
demonstration of our partners that New Zealand
has contributed, has, uh, a contribution to make and a
role to play on the global stage. These relations also
set the platform for advancing the government's ambitious,
ambitions across foreign policy trade and development
cooperation. Um, Mr Chair, the past year has seen
strong delivery against priorities set out in the government's foreign policy
reset. In the face of economic and geopolitical
headwinds, uh, the foreign policy reset ensures our resources
are directed, uh, are, are sent in the direction of efforts and
are aligned in pursuing outcomes that matter to the security and
prosperity of New Zealand. Notwithstanding these headwinds, headwinds,
we have delivered real progress across all key lines of effort in the
reset. Uh, priority relations in South and Southeast Asia
have been elevated through the comprehensive strategic partnerships with ASEAN as a
whole and with Vietnam and Singapore.
Uh, the relationship with India has seen a step change with enhanced political and
defence engagement, and good progress has been made on the bilateral
FTA negotiations following its launch in, only
just in March of this year. Similar in North Asia, the
relationship with the Republic of Korea has recently been bolstered with
the conclusion of a comprehensive strategic partnership and
high-level engagement with China, including the Prime Minister's visit
2025, has reinforced that important
relationship. There's been increased effort in supporting
resilient and prosperous Pacific demonstrated through delivery of
over $517 million NZD in development cooperation
funding in the year 24-25,
aligned with our Pacific partners priorities, continued
investment in Pacific regional institutions and humanitarian
assistance, including following the December 2024
earthquakes in Vanuatu.
We have reinvigorated our relationship with like-minded partners,
including through enhanced political engagement in Canberra, in
London, Washington and Ottawa, sustained engagement in many
lateral groups like the Indo-Pacific Four, and in
contributions to collective security challenges, including supporting
Ukraine's self-defence in the face of Russia's ongoing
agression- aggression, following their illegal invasion.
We have targeted our multilateral engagement on global issues that
matter most to New Zealand, including improving international stewardship of
oceans, promoting reform of key multilateral institutions,
upholding the Antarctic Treaty System, and contributing
to global climate action and to disarmament and
non-proliferation efforts. And we've strengthened our international
trade architecture-
(coughing)
... in support of the government's goal of achieving a significant step change in
export value over the next decade.
A new trade agreement with UAE, United Arab Emirates and an,
and an updated ASEAN-Australia-New Zealand agreement
entered into force, and negotiations were concluded with the Gulf
Cooperation Council. Our focus on full
implementation of recent trade agreements with the UK and the EU has
delivered significant growth and exports into those markets, creating
more opportunities and delivering greater returns for New Zealand.
And I want to recognise the support across the House for many of those agreements,
including the early entry into force of the EU agreement and the
significant benefits delivering for all New Zealanders, particularly
exporters. We've also demonstrated leadership and creativity in
working to sustain the international rules-based trading system, which
remains under challenge, contributing to the establishment of the
future investment and trade partnership, uh, and advancing
opportunities for deeper engagement between the EU and the
CPTPP members. These outcomes all represent
serious progress on the ambition of the reset and
reflecting the strong leadership and direction the government is
bringing to its international...... engagement.
Um, the past year has also required resilience and agility to respond to
pressing challenges beyond the reset priorities.
For example, emergency responses following natural disasters in the Pacific
and arising from conflicts in the Middle East have required the evacuation of New
Zealand citizens from dangerous situations offshore.
In the trade and economic domain, the government has worked hard to support New
Zealand businesses in navigating uncertainty, including through the impact of
US tariffs. This will remain a priority and was
positive, uh, it was positive to see a step in the right direction last month
with the removal of additional tariffs on a range of New Zealand
agricultural, uh, products in, in the US.
Being, um, positioned to manage uncertainty
is the reality we face in a more challenged world.
Advancing our priorities and defending our interests is a collective effort
across government, including my cabinet colleagues and the broad range of
New Zealand in- agencies who all play an important role in helping
deliver on the ambition of the reset.
We enhance our influence and effectiveness in the work,
uh, in the world by working collectively to address
cross-cutting challenges and develop coordinated approaches, and by
using all levers at our disposal. Uh, Mr.
Chair and, uh, committee members, I thank you for the time today.
I know that you have, uh, some hours before you, uh,
with officials. I, uh, have, uh, um, the chief executive of the
Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, Bed Corry, here with me today, and the
senior leadership team, uh, including Bengalis Vitalis, uh, who
will help, uh, me, uh, in the trade area.
Uh, but yeah, I'm very happy to hear from you.
Excellent. Thank you very much for that, Minister.
Um, can I just clarify before we get underway, which two of the three Labour
members are participating?
No, no, usually I, I thought I'd been swapped out. That's right.

I'm out.

Sorry-
(laughs)
... so you don't have to, you don't have to exclude somebody for my benefit.

First question anyway, the Honorable Peeni Henare.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Minister, officials.
Um, thank you for your comprehensive remarks, uh, to open our
session. You are right. Some of these, some of the more technical matters we can
dive into, uh, with the officials.
My first question, uh, is the big move on Pacific
reset, uh, and, uh, from the
outset, it looks like it's the right thing to do.
Yet what we're seeing across the Pacific, uh, far more
challenges, uh, arising despite the huge efforts over the
past two years, uh, with the Pacific reset.
I wondered if, Minister, you'd like to offer some views on that?
Yeah. Well, the Pacific reset is very important.
Uh, I, I would argue that, uh, one of, probably one of New Zealand's greatest
obligations as a good neighbor and a friend is to our, you know, Pacific
countries and particularly, uh, many of the smaller nations that face
challenges around climate change.
And, you know, they're not immune to the geopolitical challenges that are going on
around the world. Uh, we work closely with other
countries, uh, advocating strongly, of course, for Australia, uh,
with the US, with Europe, with most parts of the world about
the importance of engagement with the Pacific and support them.
Uh, a lot of what we do is around the development, uh, space, uh, um,
to support the nations, uh, uh, to help grow greater
self-sufficiency. But we also need to recognize, you know, whilst
geo- ge- geographical isolation in itself is a
wonderful thing when you need peace and quiet, that the Pacific
has not always been as quiet and peaceful as we are used to.
And so, we have continued to step up our efforts there.
But maybe for greater detail about some of the activities in the, the reset either
now or later on, officials could help that.
Thank you. I appreciate that.
Celia Abraham.
Um, thank you. Um, following on your question from about the
Pacific, um,
I was interested, I've got some bigger questions, but I'd just like to focus on

Yeah.

We, uh, you know, we address them respectfully as our

Well, thank you, Minister. Thank you, committee. And good morning.
Um, Bernadette may have further detail, but just to reaffirm the, the points that

also more, more tragically, um, impacted the people of
Vanuatu. Uh,
and there are, in a way, two stages to this.
There was the immediate response we had to provide...
... which is the kind of things you can imagine you need immediately after an

reconstruction, uh, that will ensue.
Um, one, one good example, for example, is, is the

And that's an area where New Zealand will be engaging in a

Yeah. Thank you very much. Good morning.
Um, and just very briefly to add, I think one of the interesting things out of this
one was, A, I was getting in very quickly, um, and particularly with our, with

will be dealing with over time. And actually, literally, uh,
literally today, our high commission team are just moving
into a, uh, a temporary, a longer term temporary
premise for them, which just shows one year on of how challenging it
has been on the ground with so much, um, so much infrastructure damage.
So, it was a, um, a, a tragedy and sadly, um, one that we have
many times over in the Pacific.
Thank you. Thank you. We share the ring of fire.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thank you, minister, and your officials for your

whether, uh, it's likely or not that we would be in breach

And as I said earlier at the Minister of Climate Change earlier this year, that
they, the good news is the, the, the, the pathway gets us there further
than, uh, 2050. Uh, uh, we take our
obligations extremely seriously in all international agreements, trade agreements

And so ultimately, um, it's a hypothetical question

The one thing I would like to clarify though is that there, there is a, a confusion

that government has authority to tell the New Zealand Parliament what to do.
If you look at those agreements, many of them are greater than aspirational, but it
is very clear it is the sovereign right of the New Zealand Parliament to make
decisions in the best interests of New Zealand.
And how we meet those obligations is actually what we are, you know, going through
and dealing with now. Y- you will have seen that the European Commissioner for
Trade and Industry, uh, was in New Zealand just quite
recently. Uh, we had a very big trade summit.
There were a lot of, uh, businesses from Europe that came down to see what more
we could do together under the free trade agreement.
He was asked the same question, it was only a week or so after the government had
announced, uh, its new, uh, uh, um, policy on how the
agriculture sector would meet its obligations and its contribution to
New Zealand being net zero by 2050.
He, to the media and subsequently in other engagement, said he
had seen the changes that New Zealand had made, he believed they were compatible
with the free trade agreement, uh, and that New Zealand meets its obligations.
And it was for us to decide how to do that.
And so, uh, uh, uh, there is no advice that I have based on the
decisions that have been made, uh, that were contraventions of the, any
agreement. And the reason for that is the government takes their obligations
seriously, and would not make a decision that would contravene the current free
trade agreement. So-
Just very quick sup- And thank you for that, uh, that explanation.
I agree that it's up to New Zealand to decide how we meet those

advice, advice in terms-
Well-
... of what our potential liability is?
Well interestingly, there's two parts of the debate.
The first is our obligation under Paris, uh, to be net zero by 2050.
The second is there are domestic targets set by the last government.
Unfortunately, when the previous government set those targets
a- around NDCs in 2030, it was already impossible at that time to meet

question that might have been asked by a previous cabinet.
However, the very good news is we are on target, on track to meet an
obligation by 2050. And therefore, to suppose what would
happen if we didn't is not an important discussion to have because
we remain on track.
Supplementary from-
No, not... It's the new primary. Thank you.
Oh, okay. Sorry. We'll come to Tim Costly first.
Yeah. My question's also on trade agreements.
Um, we've seen a, a lot of progress I think in the year,
but this one, uh, we had the Gulf States agreement

... um, touch on sort of free trade for the year in review, but also with an eye to

Yeah. Well, I suppose more broadly to start, that New Zealand's a trading nation.
You know, it's often said 400 million people around the world get 10% of their diet
from New Zealand. Uh, we produce more food and fiber than we could
consume ourselves. It is of very high quality. It has some...
You know, New Zealand producers have some of the highest quality reputation or

And the world and consumers are demanding this.
With that comes a story of provenance.
So, in everything the government does, current and previous governments, it is
about upholding that reputation. But the world is not a fair place.
People don't have to buy from us. And so-
Mm-hmm.
So ultimately, the government's job is to level the playing field.
We are blessed to have, uh, I would say arguably the best trade
negotiators in the world.... and particularly, you know, for our
slides, uh, and I know, uh, uh, that's the envy of many
other ministers that I go up against to talk about trade and we negotiate
against. You know, New Zealand has, uh, around 100 free
trade, uh, double tax or investment agreements with countries of
the world, which levels the playing field, and it gives real opportunity for New
Zealand businesses to consider risk and where they want to put
their own effort rather than the government telling them what they should do.
So, our job is to level the playing field.
Uh, well, you know, I was very pleased that we were able to reach across the House
and get the UFTA in force many, many months sooner than
had been predicted, and we did that because, one, trade should be
bipartisan. It doesn't mean we have to agree on everything, but we must

And if we don't do that, then fundamentally, we don't understand how the New

that EUFTA having entered into force early, uh, in the first 18

Yeah.

... in due course. And with India, um, uh, you know, against, uh, uh, a

a, a negotiation might not be able to take place, talk about

There's this very unusual thing happens in trade.
Uh, when two governments say they're gonna, want to negotiate a trade agreement,
generally trade starts going up.
Mm-hmm.
Uh, when they conclude, even if it's not implemented, trade goes up.
And then, of course, when it's i- cl- included in force, it can't
... That's without us doing anything.

country holding seminars and providing information, then
exponentially the opportunity through trade is more significant.
In the first year of the UKFTA, I don't remember exact

If you take the UKFTA, the EUFTA, uh, the start of

Mm-hmm. ... the tangible ge- benefit for New Zealanders is more jobs,

But in as far as a bit more detail, perhaps I'll pass to, uh, Bee

Okay. Did you-

and our advice tended towards

at the time the advice was that there were three different options the minister w-

New Zealand is respected for being considered both in foreign affairs and
